intro: Cherry is where dwarfing rootstocks changed the backyard tree. A sweet cherry on its traditional Mazzard seedling stock makes a big, slow-to-bear tree, but the modern Gisela and Krymsk clones bring it down to a pickable, early-cropping size. Mazzard and Mahaleb are the vigorous seedling stocks (Mahaleb is the one usually sold bare-root in Australia), Colt is a middleweight, and Gisela 5 is the popular garden dwarf. Soil drainage matters as much as size: some stocks need deep, free-draining ground, others cope with heavy or wet soil.
sources_note: Rootstock guidance on this page draws on Washington State University tree-fruit extension, the Royal Horticultural Society, the Dave Wilson Nursery grower comparisons, and Australian nursery rootstock listings.

sources.name: Washington State University Tree Fruit: rootstocks for cherry
Royal Horticultural Society: rootstocks for fruit
Dave Wilson Nursery: grower rootstock comparisons
Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery: Mahaleb cherry rootstock (retail, Australia)

rootstocks.name: Mazzard
rootstocks.soils: Fibrous roots cope with wet and heavy soils better than Mahaleb
rootstocks.notes: The traditional full-size sweet cherry rootstock, and the standard every other stock is measured against. Its fibrous root system suits wetter, heavier ground, but it makes a big tree that is slow to bear and is susceptible to crown gall, bacterial canker and root-lesion nematode.

rootstocks.name: Mahaleb
rootstocks.vigour: Somewhat dwarfing, but still a large tree
rootstocks.soils: Deep soils with good drainage; drought tolerant, but sensitive to waterlogging and not for heavy soils
rootstocks.notes: A cold-hardy, precocious, drought-tolerant seedling rootstock for deep, free-draining ground, and the cherry rootstock usually sold bare-root in Australia. It fails on heavy or poorly drained soils, where Mazzard or Colt do better.

rootstocks.name: Colt
rootstocks.soils: Tolerates wet, heavy soils better than Mahaleb, but drought sensitive and not cold hardy
rootstocks.notes: A middleweight stock that trims size a little versus Mazzard and copes with heavier, wetter ground. It resists bacterial canker, but is drought sensitive and not cold hardy, so it suits milder districts with reliable water.

rootstocks.name: Gisela 5
rootstocks.type: Clonal Prunus cerasus x P. canescens
rootstocks.vigour: Dwarfing, about half the size of Mazzard (2.4 to 3m)
rootstocks.soils: Does well even on heavy soil; crops so heavily it may need staking
rootstocks.notes: The popular backyard dwarf: roughly half the size of a tree on Mazzard, very precocious and quite virus tolerant, and it performs well on heavy soil. Because it crops so heavily it may need permanent support, and it can throw a few suckers.

rootstocks.name: Gisela 6
rootstocks.type: Clonal Prunus cerasus x P. canescens
rootstocks.soils: Well suited to heavy soil types
rootstocks.notes: A larger, more forgiving semi-dwarf than Gisela 5, very precocious despite the extra vigour, tolerant of many cherry viruses and not prone to suckering. A good middle path on heavy ground where a full dwarf might struggle.

rootstocks.name: Krymsk 5 and Krymsk 6
rootstocks.vigour: Size controlling, about 20 to 30% smaller than Mazzard
rootstocks.soils: Krymsk 6 tolerates water stress; adapted to a range of soils
rootstocks.notes: Precocious, non-suckering size-controlling stocks that reduce the tree by roughly 20 to 30 percent compared with Mazzard. Both are sensitive to Prunus necrotic ringspot and prune dwarf virus, so start with clean, certified trees.

grow_your_own.intro: Cherry rootstock splits into two camps, and only one can be raised at home. The vigorous seedling stocks come from stones; the dwarfing stocks that made backyard cherries practical are clones you have to buy.

sections.heading: Dwarfing cherry stock is a clone, not a seedling
sections.body: Gisela 5, Gisela 6, the Krymsk stocks and Colt are named clonal rootstocks, so they cannot be raised from a cherry stone: a stone only ever gives you a vigorous seedling with no size control. If you want a small, early-bearing cherry you buy the named dwarfing rootstock and graft your variety onto it, the same way you would with a dwarfing apple stock.

sections.heading: Seedling stock from a stone
sections.body: Mazzard (wild sweet cherry) and Mahaleb (St Lucie cherry) are seedling rootstocks, so a home grafter can raise them from cherry stones. Expect a vigorous, full-size, variable tree with no size control. Match the stock to your ground: Mazzard's fibrous roots suit wetter, heavier soil, while Mahaleb wants deep, free-draining ground and is the more drought tolerant of the two.

faqs.q: What is the best dwarfing rootstock for a cherry tree?
faqs.a: Gisela 5 is the usual backyard dwarf, about half the size of a tree on Mazzard (a trained tree around 2.4 to 3m), very precocious and virus tolerant, and it does well even on heavy soil. It crops so heavily it may need staking. For a slightly larger, more forgiving tree, Gisela 6 (about 80 to 90 percent of Mazzard) or a Krymsk stock (20 to 30 percent smaller than Mazzard) trims the size without going fully dwarf.

faqs.q: Which cherry rootstock suits heavy or wet soil?
faqs.a: Mazzard and the Gisela stocks handle heavier ground: Mazzard's fibrous roots cope with wet, heavy soil, and Gisela 5 and Gisela 6 both do well on heavy soils. Colt also tolerates wet, heavy soil better than Mahaleb. Avoid Mahaleb on heavy or poorly drained ground, because it needs deep, free-draining soil, though it is the drought-tolerant choice for deep sandy soils and the cherry rootstock usually sold bare-root in Australia.
